meta.tagline: Day 23. Trump gave Iran 48 hours to reopen Hormuz or he obliterates their power grid. Iran said it will destroy every energy site in the Gulf. Missiles hit Dimona. Cuba went dark. Sunday.
meta.greeting: Good morning, Cafe crew -- Day 23 and Trump just set a 48-hour countdown clock.

headlines: Trump gives Iran 48 hours: reopen Hormuz or power plants get obliterated
Iran missiles hit Dimona and Arad -- near Israel nuclear research center
Iran threatens to irreversibly destroy all Gulf energy infrastructure
Cuba power grid collapsed for the third time this month
Sudan hospital strike killed 64 including 13 children
Panama Canal at max capacity -- Hormuz shutdown rerouting global shipping

articles.title: Trump Gave Iran 48 Hours. Then What?
articles.excerpt: Reopen the Strait of Hormuz in 48 hours or the US will obliterate every power plant in Iran. That is not a paraphrase. That is the actual threat.
articles.body:
President Trump issued Iran a 48-hour ultimatum today: reopen the Strait of Hormuz or the United States will obliterate Iran power plants.

That is not a summary. That is the threat, word for word.

Iran responded within hours. Tehran said if the US hits its power grid, Iran will irreversibly destroy energy sites across the entire Gulf -- Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, all of it.

So the situation as of Sunday evening: one side says open the strait or we destroy your grid. The other side says touch our grid and we destroy the region.

The 48-hour clock started this morning. That puts the deadline around Tuesday morning. Oil markets open Monday. This will not be a quiet week.
articles.barista_take: 48 hours. Open Hormuz or lose your power grid. Iran said try it and we burn down the Gulf. Monday markets are going to be fun.

articles.title: Iran Hit Dimona. That Is Not a Random Target.
articles.excerpt: Iranian missiles struck Dimona and Arad -- two cities next to Israel nuclear research center. Cluster munitions landed on Tel Aviv streets. This was targeted.
articles.body:
Iran launched missiles at Dimona and Arad in southern Israel today. These are not random cities. Dimona is home to Israel nuclear research center -- the facility widely believed to house its nuclear weapons program.

Cluster munitions also landed on Tel Aviv streets. Israel defense minister said Israel will send Iran back decades in response.

Meanwhile, Iran IRGC is accused of intentionally targeting civilian population centers to pressure Israel government into halting the war. Israel says it will not work.

Day 23. Both sides are hitting targets that used to be off-limits. The escalation ladder does not have a top.
articles.barista_take: Missiles on Dimona. Cluster bombs on Tel Aviv. The word escalation needs a bigger word.

articles.title: Iran Threatened to Destroy Every Energy Site in the Gulf
articles.excerpt: If Trump hits power plants, Iran says it will irreversibly destroy energy infrastructure across Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, and Bahrain. The entire Gulf oil supply is now a bargaining chip.
articles.body:
Iran response to Trump 48-hour ultimatum was not subtle. Tehran said if the US attacks Iranian power plants, Iran will irreversibly destroy energy sites across the Gulf.

That means Saudi oil fields. UAE ports. Qatar LNG terminals. Bahrain refineries. All of it.

Bahrain has already intercepted 143 missiles and 242 drones since February 28. Saudi Arabia shot down 47 drones in a single day this week. Kuwait is under regular missile attack.

The Gulf is already under fire. Iran is saying it can get much worse. The word irreversibly is doing a lot of work in that sentence.
articles.barista_take: Iran said irreversibly. That word does not leave room for negotiation.

articles.title: Cuba Went Dark Again. Third Time This Month.
articles.excerpt: Cuba power grid collapsed Saturday -- the third total blackout in March. Decaying infrastructure plus the US oil blockade plus a Middle East war equals no electricity for 11 million people.
articles.body:
Cuba entire power grid collapsed on Saturday. The country went completely dark. This is the third total blackout in March.

The cause is a combination of decaying infrastructure that has not been updated in decades, a longstanding US embargo, and now the global oil crisis caused by the Iran war.

Eleven million people without electricity. No air conditioning. No refrigeration. No internet. In March heat.

The Iran war is not just about the Middle East. It is about oil supply chains that connect to every country that cannot generate its own power. Cuba is the canary in the coal mine.
articles.barista_take: 11 million people in the dark. Third time this month. The war is everywhere oil is.

articles.title: Monday Markets Are Going to Be a Problem
articles.excerpt: Friday close: S&P 6,506 (-1.5%). Then Trump issued a 48-hour ultimatum over the weekend. Iran threatened to destroy the Gulf. Oil futures are going to open ugly.
articles.body:
Here is what happened since markets closed Friday:

Trump gave Iran 48 hours to reopen Hormuz or he obliterates their power plants. Iran said it will destroy every energy site in the Gulf if he does. Missiles hit Dimona. Cuba went dark.

Friday close: S&P 500 at 6,506, down 1.51%. Dow off 444 points. VIX at 26.78, up 11%.

Oil futures open Monday morning. The 48-hour deadline lands Tuesday. Every energy trader on the planet is recalculating right now.

If you thought Friday was bad, Monday has not read the room yet.
articles.barista_take: Markets closed Friday scared. They are about to open Monday terrified.

articles.title: The Panama Canal Is Maxed Out. Thank Hormuz.
articles.excerpt: 36-38 ships per day through Panama. Maximum capacity. LNG tankers rerouting from Hormuz are flooding the canal. Global shipping is being rewritten in real time.
articles.body:
The Panama Canal is operating at absolute maximum capacity -- 36 to 38 vessels per day. The canal chief said this is driven by a massive increase in LNG tanker demand because the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed.

Global shipping is being rerouted in real time. Routes that used to go through Hormuz are now going around Africa or through Panama. Transit times are longer. Costs are higher. Supply chains are stretching.

There have been 21 confirmed attacks on commercial vessels and offshore infrastructure since March 1. The UK rates the Gulf threat level as critical.

The world economy runs on ships. The ships are running out of places to go.
articles.barista_take: Panama Canal at max. Hormuz closed. 21 ships attacked. Global trade is playing Tetris with fewer slots.

articles.title: Oil Opens Monday With a 48-Hour Countdown
articles.excerpt: Oil at 110 on Friday. Trump just threatened to obliterate power plants. Iran threatened to destroy the Gulf. Monday morning oil futures will price in chaos.
articles.body:
Oil closed above 110 on Friday. That was before the 48-hour ultimatum.

When futures open Monday morning, traders will be pricing in two scenarios: either Iran reopens Hormuz (oil drops) or the US hits power plants and Iran retaliates across the Gulf (oil goes to a place it has never been).

The 48-hour deadline is Tuesday morning. That means Monday is pure uncertainty. Uncertainty is the one thing oil markets hate most.

Gas is already heading to 5 dollars nationally. If the Gulf escalation happens, 5 dollars will look cheap.
articles.barista_take: Oil at 110. 48-hour ultimatum. Monday futures will be chaos. Fill up today.

articles.title: Bahrain Has Shot Down 242 Drones in 23 Days
articles.excerpt: 143 missiles. 242 drones. That is Bahrain alone since Feb 28. Saudi shot down 47 in one day. The Gulf is running an air defense marathon.
articles.body:
Bahrain has intercepted and destroyed 143 missiles and 242 drones since the war began on February 28. That is one country. Population 1.5 million.

Saudi Arabia shot down 47 drones in a single day this week, including a concentrated barrage of 38 within three hours.

These are not small numbers. Modern air defense systems were designed for occasional threats, not sustained bombardment. Every interceptor missile costs money. Every drone Iran launches costs a fraction of what it costs to shoot down.

The math is asymmetric and it favors the attacker. This is the drone war nobody planned for.
articles.barista_take: 242 drones. 143 missiles. One tiny country. Iran is stress-testing every air defense system in the Gulf.

articles.title: 21 Attacks on Ships Since March 1. The Gulf Is a War Zone.
articles.excerpt: UK maritime authority rates the Gulf threat level critical. 21 confirmed attacks on commercial vessels. Insurance rates are through the roof.
articles.body:
The UK Maritime Trade Operations announced that the threat level across the Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman remains critical.

There have been 21 confirmed attacks on commercial vessels and offshore infrastructure since March 1. That is roughly one attack per day.

Shipping insurance premiums for Gulf transits have skyrocketed. Many carriers are refusing to enter the region at all. The ones that do are charging war-risk premiums that get passed to consumers.

Every product that touches a ship -- which is most products -- is getting more expensive because of this. The war tax is invisible but it is on every receipt.
articles.barista_take: 21 ship attacks in 22 days. Insurance through the roof. You are paying the war tax at the grocery store.

articles.title: Israel Just Destroyed a Major Bridge in Lebanon
articles.excerpt: The Israeli Air Force destroyed the Qasmiya Bridge on the Litani River. A senior Hezbollah commander was killed. Lebanon death toll passed 1,000.
articles.body:
The Israeli Air Force struck the Qasmiya Bridge over the Litani River in southern Lebanon today. The bridge is on the coastal highway -- a major transportation artery.

Israel warned before the strike, then destroyed it. A senior Hezbollah Radwan Force commander was killed in a separate airstrike.

Since Israeli attacks on Lebanon escalated on March 2, the death toll has passed 1,000 with at least 2,584 wounded. Residents in southern Lebanon are receiving threatening phone calls from foreign numbers telling them to evacuate.

The war with Iran is also a war in Lebanon. Two fronts. One military.
articles.barista_take: Bridge destroyed. Commander killed. 1,000 dead in Lebanon since March 2. Two fronts, one war.

articles.title: 64 Dead in a Hospital Strike in Sudan. 13 Were Children.
articles.excerpt: A strike on a hospital in western Darfur killed 64 people including 13 children. The world is watching Iran. Sudan is watching nobody watch them.
articles.body:
At least 64 people were killed in a strike on a hospital in Sudan western Darfur region last week. Thirteen of them were children. The World Health Organization confirmed the numbers Saturday.

This story got almost no attention. The entire global news cycle is consumed by Iran, oil, and Trump ultimatums. Sudan civil war has killed tens of thousands and displaced millions. A hospital full of children was hit and it did not lead a single broadcast.

Some wars get 48-hour ultimatums. Others do not get 48 seconds of attention.
articles.barista_take: 64 dead in a hospital. 13 children. Nobody is watching. That is the other war.

articles.title: The 48-Hour Ultimatum Just Created More Jobs
articles.excerpt: Energy risk modeling. Defense logistics. Crisis communications. Every escalation cycle creates another hiring wave.
articles.body:
Every escalation cycle creates hiring waves:

Energy risk modeling -- every oil company, trading firm, and government agency needs people who can model what happens if the Gulf goes dark. These roles did not exist six months ago. They pay six figures.

Defense logistics -- 242 drones intercepted by Bahrain alone. Somebody has to supply, maintain, and coordinate that. Cleared logistics roles are everywhere.

Crisis communications -- every multinational with Gulf exposure needs comms teams who can respond to 48-hour ultimatums at 2am. PR firms specializing in geopolitical crisis are hiring aggressively.

The war economy is hiring faster than tech right now. Different skills. Same urgency.
articles.barista_take: Energy risk. Defense logistics. Crisis comms. The 48-hour ultimatum just posted three job listings.

articles.title: Day 23. Your Brain Cannot Sustain This.
articles.excerpt: 48-hour ultimatums. Power grid threats. Hospital strikes. Your cortisol has been elevated for over three weeks. One hour offline is not optional anymore.
articles.body:
Day 23 of sustained crisis news. Your cortisol -- the stress hormone -- has been elevated for over three weeks straight.

Research shows that chronic cortisol elevation degrades memory, slows decision-making, weakens immune function, and disrupts sleep. After three weeks, these effects compound.

You cannot outwork your own biology. One hour offline today is not self-care. It is maintenance. Like changing oil in a car. Skip it and the engine breaks.

The 48-hour ultimatum will still exist after your hour. Your ability to think clearly about it will be better.
articles.barista_take: 23 days of cortisol. Your brain is running on fumes. One hour offline. Non-negotiable.

articles.title: Monday Is Going to Be Rough. Prep Your Sleep Tonight.
articles.excerpt: Markets open Monday into a 48-hour ultimatum. Tonight is your last good sleep for a while. Protect it.
articles.body:
Monday is going to be intense. Oil futures open into a 48-hour countdown. Markets are going to react to the weekend escalation. Every news cycle will be dominated by the ultimatum.

Tonight is your prep night. Two things:

First: no screens after 10pm. The blue light plus the anxiety content is a double hit on your melatonin.

Second: the 10-minute brain dump. Write every worry on paper before bed. Not to solve them. To get them out of your head.

Monday will demand your best thinking. Give your brain the best shot by sleeping well tonight.
articles.barista_take: Monday will be chaos. Tonight is prep night. No screens after 10. Brain dump on paper. Sleep.

articles.title: The Sunday Planning Hour That Saves Your Whole Week
articles.excerpt: One hour on Sunday. Three priorities for the week. Written before the 48-hour news cycle swallows your calendar.
articles.body:
One hour. Sunday afternoon. Three things:

Write your three most important tasks for the week. Not the most urgent. The most important. The ones that move your life forward even if the news is on fire.

Block 90 minutes on Monday morning for your number one priority. Before email. Before Slack. Before the news cycle takes over.

Review last week. What did you actually finish? Most people forget their wins by Sunday. Write them down. Your brain needs the evidence.

The 48-hour ultimatum will dominate Monday. Your planning hour makes sure it does not dominate your output.
articles.barista_take: Sunday planning hour. Three priorities. 90-minute Monday block. The news will scream. Your plan whispers louder.

articles.title: The 2-Check News Diet That Keeps You Informed Without Drowning
articles.excerpt: Check news twice a day. 8am and 6pm. 15 minutes each. You will miss nothing important and gain back 2 hours of focus.
articles.body:
You do not need to check the news every 20 minutes. The 48-hour ultimatum will not resolve because you refreshed Twitter.

The 2-check protocol: read news at 8am and 6pm. Fifteen minutes each. That is it.

Everything important will be in those two windows. Nothing that happens between 8:15am and 5:59pm requires your real-time attention unless you work in news or defense.

The average person checks news 17 times per day. Each check costs 5-10 minutes of refocus time. That is 2+ hours of lost productivity per day. For information you could have gotten in two 15-minute blocks.

Two checks. Thirty minutes total. You miss nothing. You gain back your day.
articles.barista_take: 8am and 6pm. 15 minutes each. You miss nothing. You gain back 2 hours. The war will still be there.

articles.title: A 48-Hour Ultimatum Is Content Gold
articles.excerpt: Trump just gave everyone a 48-hour countdown. That is two days of content. Explain what happens if Hormuz opens. Explain what happens if it does not.
articles.body:
Trump just gave you the perfect content framework: a 48-hour countdown with two outcomes.

Scenario A: Iran reopens Hormuz. Oil drops. Markets rally. Gas stabilizes. Write what that looks like.

Scenario B: The US hits power plants. Iran retaliates across the Gulf. Oil goes parabolic. Write what that looks like.

Post both on LinkedIn tomorrow morning. Scenario A vs Scenario B. Tag three people. Ask which one they think happens.

Engagement on scenario-based content is 3-5x higher than regular posts. People love choosing sides. The news just gave you the perfect format.

45 minutes. Two scenarios. One post. Maximum engagement.
articles.barista_take: 48-hour ultimatum = 48 hours of content. Scenario A vs B. Post it. Let people argue. Engagement gold.

articles.title: You Cannot Control the Countdown. Control Your Morning.
articles.excerpt: 48 hours. Power plant threats. Gulf on fire. Hospital strikes. None of it is yours to control. Your Sunday evening and Monday morning -- those are yours.
articles.body:
48-hour ultimatum. Power plant threats. Missiles on Dimona. Cuba in the dark. 64 dead in a hospital nobody is watching.

None of that is in your control. Not one piece of it.

Your Sunday evening is in your control. Your sleep tonight. Your Monday morning plan. Whether you open the news app first or the work that actually matters.

The countdown will tick whether you watch it or not. But the hour you spend planning instead of scrolling -- that hour compounds. That is the hour that separates people who survived 2026 from people who just watched it happen.
articles.quote: You cannot calm the storm. So stop trying. What you can do is calm yourself.
articles.barista_take: The countdown ticks whether you watch or not. Use the morning before the news takes it.

on_this_day.text: FDR signed the Beer and Wine Revenue Act, ending Prohibition. America celebrated with a drink. After today's news, same energy.
Comet Hale-Bopp made its closest approach to Earth. 29 years later, we are watching a different kind of fireball.
Reese Witherspoon turned 50. William Shatner turned 95. At least something normal happened today.

lines: Iran: Touch our grid and we burn down the Gulf.
Panama Canal: we are full, thanks.
meme.closer: It is Sunday. The countdown started anyway.

cafe_wins: One hour offline -- no news, no doomscrolling
Write your three priorities for the week
Block 90 minutes Monday morning for deep work
Brain dump on paper before bed
